Patterns and Classes
A pattern is a vector of measured features:
x =

Our goal is to assign each unclassiﬁed pattern x to one of a set
of classes {ωi}.

Feature Spaces, Prototypes, Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers
Feature Spaces
Can think of each pattern as a point in a feature space:
Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers, Linear Discriminants
Feature Spaces, Prototypes, Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers
Feature Spaces
Key ideas:
▶Patterns from the same class should cluster together in
feature space
▶Supervised training: learn the properties of the cluster
(distribution) for each class
▶Unsupervised training: ﬁnd the clusters from scratch

title: Page 3
text:
Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers, Linear Discriminants
Feature Spaces, Prototypes, Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers
Minimum-Distance Classiﬁer
▶Idea:
Use a single prototype for each class ωi
(usually the class’s mean mi)
▶Training:
Just calculate each class’s prototype (mean)
▶Classiﬁcation:
Assign unlabeled patterns to the nearest prototype in the
feature space
Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers, Linear Discriminants
Decision Boundaries
Decision Boundaries
If we partition the feature space according to the nearest
prototype, we create decision boundaries:

Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers, Linear Discriminants
Decision Boundaries
Simpler Calculation
Key idea:
▶We don’t really need to know the distances to the
prototypes—we just want to know which is closest.
▶Any monotonic function of the distance will also do:
scalar multiplication, log, exponent, reciprocal, negation, ...

Minimum-Distance Classiﬁers, Linear Discriminants
Discriminants
Discriminants
In general, a function used to test class membership is called a
discriminant.
Three approaches for multiple classes:
1. Construct a single discriminant for each class that
separates ωi from “not ωi”.
2. Construct a discriminant gij between each pair of classes
ωi and ωj:
gij(x) = gi(x) −gj(x)
3. Construct a single discriminant gi(x) for each class ωi, and
assign x to class ωi if gi(x) > gj(x) for all other classes ωj.
